skill.name: pdf-processing
skill.description: Extract text and tables from PDF files, fill forms, merge documents, split pages, and save processed PDF data.
skill.summary: Process PDFs with extraction, form filling, merging, splitting, and table export examples.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-crypto alerts were false positives from markdown code fences and descriptive text, not executable shell or cryptographic code. The skill contains local file-write examples for PDF outputs, text, and CSV files, so it should disclose filesystem effects but shows no malicious intent, network access, credential handling, or prompt injection.

low_findings.title: Local File Writes in Processing Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples that write filled PDFs, flattened PDFs, extracted text, and CSV output to local files. This is expected for PDF processing, but users should choose output paths carefully to avoid overwriting files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-write calls are directly visible in Python examples. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, so the security impact is low.

low_findings.title: Static Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick alerts correspond to markdown code fences around Python examples. No shell command execution, subprocess use, or user-controlled command construction was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are markdown delimiters for Python blocks. Manual review found no external command APIs or shell syntax.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert points to metadata text, and the reconnaissance alert points to email validation error handling. No cryptographic operation, host inspection, environment probing, or system reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain a skill description and a validation error string. They do not implement crypto or inspect system state.

content.user_title: Process PDFs with Extraction and Form Tools
content.value_statement: PDF workflows often require manual copying, page handling, and form updates. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical Python patterns for extracting, filling, merging, splitting, and exporting PDF content.
actual_capabilities: Extract text from one page or every page in a PDF.
Extract detected tables from PDF pages and export them to CSV.
Fill PDF form fields with provided values.
Flatten filled PDF forms for distribution.
Merge several PDF files into one output document.
Split selected page ranges into a new PDF.
limitations: Scanned PDFs require OCR tools such as Tesseract before text extraction works.
Table extraction depends on PDF layout quality and may need manual cleanup.
Form filling requires exact field names from the source PDF.
Examples write local output files and may overwrite existing paths.

use_cases.title: Extract report data
use_cases.description: Pull text and tables from financial, operations, or research PDFs for review and reuse.

use_cases.title: Prepare completed forms
use_cases.description: Identify fields, fill known values, validate required entries, and flatten the final document.

use_cases.title: Assemble document packets
use_cases.description: Merge related PDFs or split selected pages into clean files for sharing.

prompt_templates.title: Extract first page text
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pdf-processing skill to extract readable text from the first page of my PDF. Show the result and note if the page appears scanned.

prompt_templates.title: Export tables
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pdf-processing skill to find tables in this PDF and export the first useful table to a CSV file with a clear output name.

prompt_templates.title: Fill and flatten a form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pdf-processing skill to inspect this PDF form, list its fields, fill the fields from my provided values, validate required fields, and create a flattened copy.

prompt_templates.title: Build a document workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the pdf-processing skill to extract text from all pages, export any tables, split pages 2 through 5, and merge the selected output with another PDF.

output_examples.input: A user provides a three-page invoice PDF and asks for the text.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns page-by-page extracted text and flags any page where no selectable text was found.

output_examples.input: A user provides a fillable onboarding form and a list of values.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists detected fields, reports missing required values, creates a filled copy, and explains where the flattened version was saved.

output_examples.input: A user asks to combine three signed PDF files into one packet.
output_examples.output: The assistant creates a merged PDF in the requested order and confirms the output filename.

best_practices: Work on copies of original PDFs before filling, splitting, or flattening.
Inspect form field names before mapping user data into a PDF.
Check extracted text and tables for layout errors before using them.
anti_patterns: Do not assume scanned PDFs contain extractable text without OCR.
Do not overwrite source PDFs with generated output files.
Do not trust extracted tables without reviewing row and column alignment.

faq.question: Can this skill read text from PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes. It provides pdfplumber examples for extracting text from one page or all pages.

faq.question: Can it process scanned documents?
faq.answer: Only with OCR support. The skill mentions OCR packages, but scanned PDFs need Tesseract setup.

faq.question: Can it fill PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to inspect form fields, fill values, validate data, and flatten forms.

faq.question: Does it send PDF data to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed files only show local Python library examples.

faq.question: Which Python libraries does it use?
faq.answer: It references pdfplumber, pypdf, pdf2image, pytesseract, PyPDF2, and pdfrw.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with low risk. Users should understand that examples can create or overwrite local output files.
